specification.topic-statement: The file reading utility reads the complete contents of a file from the filesystem into a null-terminated in-memory buffer.

scope.in-scope: Opening a file, determining its size, reading all bytes into a heap-allocated buffer, and null-terminating the result.
scope.boundary: This topic opens, seeks, reads, and closes a file by path. Any operation that fails produces an I/O error returned to the caller.
The output buffer is allocated as a child of the provided context. The caller is responsible for managing its lifetime through talloc.

field.name: content
field.note: heap-allocated, null-terminated, contains all file bytes followed by a null byte

field.name: size
field.note: number of bytes in the file, not counting the null terminator; only written if out_size was provided

reading-a-file.open: The file is opened in binary read mode. If the open fails, an I/O error is returned and no further operations are attempted.
reading-a-file.size-determination: The file position is seeked to the end to determine file size. If the seek fails, the file is closed and an I/O error is returned. The byte offset at the end is retrieved. If that operation fails, the file is closed and an I/O error is returned. The position is then seeked back to the beginning. If that seek fails, the file is closed and an I/O error is returned.
reading-a-file.overflow-check:
If the file size exceeds the maximum unsigned 32-bit value minus one, the file is closed and an I/O error is returned indicating the file is too large.
The overflow boundary is UINT_MAX (4,294,967,295) rather than SIZE_MAX. On 64-bit systems where SIZE_MAX is much larger, this artificially limits the maximum readable file size to approximately 4 GB.
reading-a-file.buffer-allocation: A buffer of size plus one bytes is allocated. Allocation failure terminates the process immediately.
reading-a-file.read:
All bytes are read into the buffer. The file is closed immediately after the read regardless of success. If the number of bytes read does not equal the expected file size, an I/O error is returned.
When a short read is detected, an I/O error is returned but the allocated buffer is not freed. The allocated memory remains attached to the provided context until that context is freed.
reading-a-file.null-termination: A null byte is written immediately after the last byte of file content. The content pointer and file size are written to the caller's output parameters. The size output parameter is only written if it was provided by the caller. The result carries the content pointer.
